front: Stock A has bid/ask <InlineFormula>40/42.1</InlineFormula> and Stock B has bid/ask <InlineFormula>22/23.5</InlineFormula>. front: For <BlockFormula>Y_t = 5 + 0.6Y_{t-1} + 0.3\varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t</BlockFormula> with last observed price <InlineFormula>Y_5 = 11.8</InlineFormula> and last observed error <InlineFormula>\varepsilon_5 = 0.2</InlineFormula>, the projected stock price on date 7 is about <Blank id="1"></Blank>.

front: Given prices <InlineFormula>100, 102, 105, 103, 104, 106</InlineFormula> from Day 0 to Day 5, the 3-day EMA on Day 5 with smoothing factor <InlineFormula>0.5</InlineFormula> is <Blank id="1"></Blank>.

front: For a GARCH(1,1) model with <InlineFormula>\omega = 2.5579 \times 10^{-5}</InlineFormula>, <InlineFormula>\alpha_1 = 0.2</InlineFormula>, <InlineFormula>\beta_1 = 0.7</InlineFormula>, <InlineFormula>a_{t-1} = 0.03</InlineFormula>, and <InlineFormula>\sigma_{t-1} = 0.02</InlineFormula>, the conditional variance update is about <Blank id="1"></Blank>.
back:
<Answer id="1">0.00049</Answer>

<BlockFormula>\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 a_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>= 2.5579 \times 10^{-5} + 0.2 \times 0.03^2 + 0.7 \times 0.02^2</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>= 2.5579 \times 10^{-5} + 0.00018 + 0.00028 \approx 0.00049</BlockFormula>
Reasoning: this is the direct conditional variance forecast implied by the supplied lagged shock and lagged volatility.

front: For the same GARCH(1,1) model with <InlineFormula>\omega = 2.5579 \times 10^{-5}</InlineFormula>, <InlineFormula>\alpha_1 = 0.2</InlineFormula>, and <InlineFormula>\beta_1 = 0.7</InlineFormula>, the unconditional variance of <InlineFormula>a_t</InlineFormula> is about <Blank id="1"></Blank>.
back:
<Answer id="1">0.00026</Answer>

<BlockFormula>\mathrm{Var}(a_t) = \frac{\omega}{1 - \alpha_1 - \beta_1} = \frac{2.5579 \times 10^{-5}}{1 - 0.2 - 0.7}</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>= \frac{2.5579 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = 0.00025579 \approx 0.00026</BlockFormula>
Reasoning: for GARCH(1,1), the unconditional variance exists when <InlineFormula>\alpha_1 + \beta_1 < 1</InlineFormula>.

front: If <InlineFormula>R \sim N(0.02, 0.04^2)</InlineFormula> for a <InlineFormula>10{,}000</InlineFormula> investment, the 1-month <InlineFormula>99\%</InlineFormula> relative VaR is about <Blank id="1"></Blank>.
back:
<Answer id="1">728</Answer>

<BlockFormula>\mathrm{VaR}_{0.99} = (z_{0.99}\sigma - \mu)W_0 = (2.32 \times 0.04 - 0.02) \times 10{,}000</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>= (0.0928 - 0.02) \times 10{,}000 = 728</BlockFormula>
Reasoning: relative VaR subtracts the mean return before scaling by portfolio value.

front: With account balance <InlineFormula>25{,}000</InlineFormula>, risk per trade <InlineFormula>1\%</InlineFormula>, point value <InlineFormula>50</InlineFormula> dollars per standard lot, and stop-loss <InlineFormula>40</InlineFormula> points, the position size is <Blank id="1"></Blank> standard lots.

front: With current <InlineFormula>Q(1,1)=10</InlineFormula>, reward <InlineFormula>r=50</InlineFormula>, learning rate <InlineFormula>\alpha=0.1</InlineFormula>, discount factor <InlineFormula>\gamma=0.9</InlineFormula>, and next-state max Q-value <InlineFormula>30</InlineFormula>, the updated <InlineFormula>Q(1,1)</InlineFormula> is <Blank id="1"></Blank>.

front: For Asset A with <InlineFormula>E(R_A)=8\%</InlineFormula>, <InlineFormula>\sigma_A=12\%</InlineFormula>, Asset B with <InlineFormula>E(R_B)=6\%</InlineFormula>, <InlineFormula>\sigma_B=8\%</InlineFormula>, and correlation <InlineFormula>\rho_{AB}=0.3</InlineFormula>, the minimum-variance weights are Asset A <Blank id="1"></Blank> and Asset B <Blank id="2"></Blank>. The portfolio's expected return is <Blank id="3"></Blank>, and its volatility is <Blank id="4"></Blank>.
back:
- <Answer id="1">0.2340</Answer>
- <Answer id="2">0.7660</Answer>
- <Answer id="3">6.4681%</Answer>
- <Answer id="4">7.4674%</Answer>

<BlockFormula>w_A = \frac{\sigma_B^2 - \rho \sigma_A \sigma_B}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 - 2\rho \sigma_A \sigma_B} = \frac{0.08^2 - 0.3 \times 0.12 \times 0.08}{0.12^2 + 0.08^2 - 2 \times 0.3 \times 0.12 \times 0.08} \approx 0.2340</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>w_B = 1 - w_A \approx 0.7660</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>E(R_p) = 0.2340 \times 8\% + 0.7660 \times 6\% \approx 6.4681\%</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>\sigma_p = \sqrt{w_A^2\sigma_A^2 + w_B^2\sigma_B^2 + 2w_Aw_B\rho\sigma_A\sigma_B} \approx 7.4674\%</BlockFormula>
Reasoning: the minimum-variance portfolio tilts toward the lower-volatility asset B.

front: With the same two assets and risk-free rate <InlineFormula>R_f=1\%</InlineFormula>, the tangency-portfolio weights are Asset A <Blank id="1"></Blank> and Asset B <Blank id="2"></Blank>. The portfolio's expected return is <Blank id="3"></Blank>, and its volatility is <Blank id="4"></Blank>.
back:
- <Answer id="1">0.3696</Answer>
- <Answer id="2">0.6304</Answer>
- <Answer id="3">6.7393%</Answer>
- <Answer id="4">7.6503%</Answer>

<BlockFormula>\mathbf{w} \propto \Sigma^{-1}(\mu - R_f\mathbf{1})</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>\mu - R_f\mathbf{1} = \begin{bmatrix}0.08-0.01 \\ 0.06-0.01\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.07 \\ 0.05\end{bmatrix}</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>\Sigma^{-1}(\mu - R_f\mathbf{1}) \propto \begin{bmatrix}3.6248 \\ 6.1813\end{bmatrix} \Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3696 \\ 0.6304\end{bmatrix}</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>E(R_p) \approx 6.7393\%,\quad \sigma_p \approx 7.6503\%</BlockFormula>
Reasoning: the tangency portfolio maximizes the Sharpe ratio rather than minimizing variance alone.

front: Starting from a <InlineFormula>50\%/50\%</InlineFormula> allocation, moving to the tangency weights requires turnover of <Blank id="1"></Blank> and costs about <Blank id="2"></Blank> of portfolio value at a <InlineFormula>0.1\%</InlineFormula> transaction-cost rate. The Sharpe ratio improves only from <Blank id="3"></Blank> to <Blank id="4"></Blank>, so transaction costs generally <Blank id="5"></Blank> the case for immediate rebalancing.
back:
- <Answer id="1">26.07%</Answer>
- <Answer id="2">0.0261%</Answer>
- <Answer id="3">0.7363</Answer>
- <Answer id="4">0.7502</Answer>
- <Answer id="5">weaken</Answer>

<BlockFormula>\text{Turnover} = |0.3696 - 0.5| + |0.6304 - 0.5| = 0.2607 = 26.07\%</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>\text{Cost} = 26.07\% \times 0.1\% = 0.0261\%\text{ of portfolio value}</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>\text{Sharpe}_{50/50} = \frac{7\% - 1\%}{8.1486\%} \approx 0.7363</BlockFormula>
<BlockFormula>\text{Sharpe}_{\mathrm{tan}} = \frac{6.7393\% - 1\%}{7.6503\%} \approx 0.7502</BlockFormula>
Reasoning: the Sharpe improvement is real but small, so even modest trading costs reduce the urgency of rebalancing.
